YOM Bundle Recommender System
  • Glossary
  • Edit on GitHub

Glossary

Explanation of key concepts used in the documentation.

Core Model Terms

Anchor Product

The product that a potential customer has already selected or is considering.

Example: When a customer takes Milk (anchor), the system recommends Bread, Butter, Yogurt.

—

Bundle

Combination of items sold together or recommended for joint purchase.

Example: Milk + Bread + Butter = Bundle

—

Candidate Product

Item that the system can recommend alongside the anchor product.

Example: If anchor = Milk, then candidates = [Bread, Butter, Yogurt, …]

—

Kiosk (Point of Sale)

Physical point of sale or register where the system must make recommendations.

Example: Kiosk ID 1001 = Register №1 in Moscow

—

Model Predictions

Main source of recommendations — output of trained LightGBM ranking model.

Example: Model gives score 0.95 for pair (Milk, Bread)

—

Fallback

Alternative source of recommendations when main model cannot provide result.

Levels: 1. Model — LightGBM predictions (primary) 2. MBA — Market Basket Analysis rules 3. Category Popular — Popular items in category 4. Global Popular — Top items on platform

—

Machine Learning Terms

Learning-to-Rank

Type of ML task where you must order a list of elements from relevant to irrelevant.

In our case: Order list of 100 candidates by purchase probability.

—

LambdaRank

Ranking algorithm used in LightGBM. Directly optimizes NDCG metric.

Why we use it: Most important is top-5 recommendation quality, and LambdaRank optimizes this.

—

LightGBM

Gradient Boosting Machine library. Fast and efficient for training on large datasets.

Alternatives: XGBoost, CatBoost

—

NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain)

Metric for evaluating recommendation quality on top-K positions.

Example: - NDCG@5: quality on top-5 recommendations - NDCG = 1.0: perfect - NDCG = 0.5: medium - NDCG = 0.0: poor

Formula: NDCG = DCG / IDCG

where DCG = Σ(2^rel_i - 1) / log2(i+1)

—

Feature

Numerical variable used by model for decision-making.

Examples: - anchor_price_avg (average price of anchor product) - cooc_frequency (how often purchased together) - price_ratio (ratio of prices)

Total used: 25 features

—

Label (Target Variable)

Correct answer for training example. In our case 0 or 1.

Example: - Label = 1: if anchor was purchased, then candidate was also purchased within 7 days - Label = 0: otherwise

—

Train/Val/Test Split

Division of data into 3 parts:

  • Train (80%): used for model training

  • Val (10%): for early stopping, hyperparameter selection

  • Test (10%): final quality evaluation

Important: Split by time, not randomly!

—

Feature Engineering

Process of creating new features from raw data.

Examples: - From timestamps → days_since_first_sale - From purchase history → purchase_frequency_30d - From two features → price_ratio

Why: Good features are often more important than fancy algorithms

—

Feature Scaling / Normalization

Transformation of features to same scale (e.g., 0-1).

Why: L1/L2 regularization works better when all features are on same scale

—

Feature Selection

Choice of only most important features from all available.

Why: Faster training, simpler model, lower overfitting risk

—

Overfitting

When model learns training patterns too well but doesn’t generalize to new data.

Signs: Train NDCG = 0.95, Test NDCG = 0.60

Solution: Increase regularization, reduce model complexity

—

Underfitting

When model is too simple and cannot learn needed patterns.

Signs: Train NDCG = 0.50, Test NDCG = 0.48

Solution: Increase model complexity, reduce regularization

—

System Architecture

Offline ML Inference (Batch Scoring)

Pre-computing recommendations for all possible combinations (kiosk, anchor) beforehand, instead of online inference.

Advantages: - Low latency (<2ms) - Predictable behavior - Controlled resource usage

Disadvantages: - Must pre-compute all combinations

—

Online ML Inference

Computing recommendations in real-time when request arrives.

Advantages: - No need to pre-compute everything - Immediately reflects new data

Disadvantages: - High latency (100ms+) - Unpredictable performance - Expensive (requires powerful servers)

—

4-Level Fallback Architecture

When main model predictions are unavailable, system tries alternatives in order:

  1. Level 1 (Model): LightGBM predictions if available

  2. Level 2 (MBA): Market Basket Analysis rules

  3. Level 3 (Category Popular): Most bought items in same category

  4. Level 4 (Global Popular): Most bought items overall (always available)

Why: Ensures service always returns something (even if not perfect)

—

Cold Start Problem

When system lacks data to make good recommendations for new products/kiosks.

Example: New product just added, no purchase history

Solutions: - Use popularity-based recommendations - Use category-based recommendations - Use hybrid approaches with user similarity

—

Production Terms

A/B Testing

Comparing two model versions on real users.

Example: - Group A: See recommendations from old model - Group B: See recommendations from new model - Measure which group makes more purchases

—

Monitoring

Continuous observation of system metrics in production.

Metrics: - Latency (p50, p99) - Error rate - Fallback distribution - CTR (if available)

—

SLA (Service Level Agreement)

Obligations for service availability and quality.

Example: “99.9% uptime, <10ms latency on 99% of requests”

—

Lambda Function / Serverless

Cloud function that runs on demand without managing servers.

Advantages: - Pay only for actual usage - Auto-scaling - No server management

Disadvantages: - Cold start latency - Limited runtime

—

Parquet File Format

Columnar data format optimized for analytical queries.

Advantages over CSV: - Much smaller file size (compression) - Faster to read specific columns - Better for big data

Used for: Storing pre-computed recommendations

—

Data Terms

S3 (Simple Storage Service)

AWS object storage service. Like a distributed file system.

Used for: Storing models, features, parquet files

—

Market Basket Analysis (MBA)

Analysis of which products are bought together.

Example: “People who buy Milk often buy Bread” → confidence = 0.75

Method: Apriori algorithm, association rules

—

Cooccurrence Frequency

How often two products are purchased together in same order.

Example: (Milk, Bread) appears in 850 orders out of 10,000 → frequency = 0.085

Use: Feature in ranking model

—

Abbreviations

API          Application Programming Interface
AWS          Amazon Web Services
CTR          Click-Through Rate
DCG          Discounted Cumulative Gain
IDCG         Ideal Discounted Cumulative Gain
LGBM         LightGBM
MBA          Market Basket Analysis
ML           Machine Learning
NDCG         Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain
OOM          Out of Memory
P99          99th percentile
P50          50th percentile (median)
SLA          Service Level Agreement
S3           Simple Storage Service
JSON         JavaScript Object Notation
CSV          Comma-Separated Values
XGBoost      Extreme Gradient Boosting
CatBoost     Categorical Boosting
RankNet      Ranking Neural Network
LambdaMART   Lambda Multiplicative Additive Regression Trees
REST         Representational State Transfer
HTTP         HyperText Transfer Protocol
